tweets.text:
great article!

> So, is a passkey a hardware wallet integrated into your phone? … the honest answer is no – it’s closer to a password manager

i’m gonna have to push back on this. i think the real question is: what is the threat model that real hardware wallets protect against?

in practice i never considered consumer hardware wallets like trezor or ledger safe against physical attacks. as far as i’m concerned if the physical device gets stolen, at best i have some amount of limited time to move the coins before the attacker gains access. i wouldn’t recommend to anyone to assume otherwise, and if they need stronger guarantees then they should probably use multisig

the thing that hardware wallets do defend against pretty well is some forms of phishing attacks (by educating the users not to type their seed phrase anywhere), fairly good defense from malware, and also much reduced risk of the vendor itself pushing a compromised update

passkeys are strictly superior on all these fronts:

their phishing resistance is best-in-class (sadly, implementing them naively crypto wallets negates some of the benefits but that’s a discussion for another day. even naive implementations are more phishing resistant than a hardware wallet tho)

they never expose key material to the end user, under any circumstance. this part is fantastic and ledger/trezor cannot compete. leaking your private key due to human error is 100x more common than having your device physically accessed by an attacker, and passkeys protect against that beautifully

the entire recovery path is very reasonable imo unless you’re really storing very large amounts, in which case yeah, you probably need something more specialized than consumer hardware anyway. apple implements very strong best practices in to the recovery story and i doubt most consumer hardware users get anywhere near that. they just lose their keys. which is also 100x more common than having their devices physically accessed by attackers.

the risk of having the vendor (apple) push compromised updates is also virtually zero, and the risk of being attacked by malware on your device is really low even if the keys aren’t stored inside the actual enclave. just don’t jailbreak. but you shouldn’t jailbreak your trezor either…

so yeah, i think it actually protects keys better than hardware wallets in some ways. and it definitely does a much better job than storing your keys in a password manager and just manually typing them into apps, which is a recipe for disaster unless you’re an expert (who never slips up)

unfortunately most passkey implementations i’ve seen for crypto wallets are… not very good imo. they tend to try to turn passkeys into something they’re not. using them correctly requires adjusting the usual crypto wallets paradigm quite a bit. but i think it can be done

tweets.text:
Prediction markets were supposed to be humanity's "truth machines," a way to forecast everything from elections to clinical trials.

Instead, they became sports books. Here's why, and how AI fixes it:

1. Economist Robin Hanson pitched the idea in 1990: betting markets could aggregate information better than democracy itself. Vitalik later called this "Info Finance," using money to surface truth.

2. Reality today: ~65% of @Kalshi and @Polymarket volume is sports betting. Science and research markets? Just 1.2%. The truth machine mostly predicts football.

3. The problem is who actually trades on these platforms. You need two types of people: smart traders with real information, and casual gamblers betting for fun. Smart traders only show up if gamblers are there to lose money to them. Gamblers only want quick, exciting bets. So everything defaults to sports.

4. Long-term investors don't fit here either. Prediction markets are zero-sum, meaning one person wins only if another loses. No wealth is created, so they're useless for growing capital like stocks or bonds.

5. Market creators can't fix it by funding markets themselves. The information gets leaked publicly to free-riders, and research shows ~3% of Polymarket accounts capture 30%+ of total gains. Easier to just pay informed people directly for their take.

6. Even smart traders skip long-term bets. Why lock up money for a year for a 5% edge when government bonds pay 6% risk-free?

7. AI agents change everything. They cost almost nothing to run, can be forced to participate in niche markets, behave rationally, handle private data, and explain their reasoning. Vitalik thinks this makes even tiny $10 markets viable.

8. This unlocks private corporate markets. HP beat sales forecasts by 40%. Eli Lilly picked winning drugs. Google ran 100+ markets. All died from internal politics, not bad accuracy. AI sidesteps that entirely.

9. The future of info finance isn't retail betting on the Super Bowl. It's AI-driven private markets answering questions that actually matter.

The US-Iran deal currently appears stuck in limbo. What does that mean for #uranium, #silver and other commodities? Here are my Sunday thoughts 👇

Right now both sides seem to be dragging their feet, hoping the other side blinks first and accepts a worse deal. Domestic optics matter. Nobody wants to look weak.

Without a little humility and compromise, a real agreement looks difficult.

If this drags on:
• Oil prices likely continue higher
• Inflation pressures return
• Commodity volatility increases

Normally ~20–21 million barrels/day pass through Hormuz. But the actual supply shock may be smaller than many expect because:
• 6–8 million bpd can bypass Hormuz through pipelines
• Some shipping still continues
• Demand destruction reduces consumption
• Strategic reserves are released
• Some producers increase output
So the world can partially adapt — but not without pain, and it takes time.

Gold and silver sold off initially, but that looks more like a liquidity event than a change in fundamentals.

Silver remains in a structural deficit.
Industrial demand from:
• Solar
• EVs
• Grid expansion
• Electrification
continues to grow aggressively.
And if inflation rises while real rates remain negative, capital will continue flowing toward hard assets.

Uranium is even more interesting.
The uranium equation simply does not work long-term:
Demand cannot exceed supply forever.

Unlike silver miners, most uranium producers still do NOT enjoy huge margins at current prices. Outside of Cameco and Kazakhstan, the industry is nowhere near incentive pricing.

In my view, we likely need uranium above $150/lb to truly incentivize large-scale greenfield Tier-2 development.

Bottom line:
• If a US-Iran deal happens → uranium & silver probably spike
• If tensions continue → they may spike even harder later, though volatility remains brutal short-term
I’m also extremely bullish:
#Copper
#Potash
and of course fractal graphene, the supermaterial that will transform an infinite amount of industries and products.
We are in a commodity supercycle, current volatility doesn´t change that.
My top picks below 👇

It’s almost funny how obvious this $LAB scam is, yet nobody seems to be doing anything about it.

The token is up another 2x this week and is now trading at an $8.6 BILLION FDV.

It’s pretty clear that shady market makers are painting the chart to lure in retail traders and then extract liquidity from them. The sad part is that something like this is only possible with the supportof CEXs, since you’d need fee-exempt accounts to pull it off at scale. Otherwise, the costs would be far too high.

For example, if you look at DEXs like PancakeSwap, there’s so little liquidity for a token supposedly worth nearly 11-digits that a $10,000 buy order would move the price by more than 70%. 

That’s absurd.

One thing is certain: there will always be shady founders trying to get rich through scams. That’s just the unfortunate reality of human nature, and it’s a problem that exists in every industry.

What I find even more disappointing is that major industry players like @Gate, @kucoincom, and @bitget are willing to facilitate this kind of activity just to make a quick buck, even though it damages the very industry they operate in. It shows how short-term and purely profit-driven their thinking is without any moral.

Use DEXs or you’re part of the problem.

Both were born in the midst of a bear market and grew throughout it through relentless execution by their stellar teams.

Both are now printing revenue and outperforming the market like there’s no tomorrow.

Kudos to everyone who managed to get in early on one of these rare winners in this market.

Massive respect to those who’ve been on both.

OpenAI Robotics is hiring, looking for exceptional full-stack hardware, ops, systems, and ML engineers to help us program and manufacture robots that are useful for society.

AI should be able to help people in the physical world. In the short term, we are focused on robots to support skilled workers to build our future infrastructure; in the long term, we imagine everyone having a personal robot doing anything they need.

Our world simulation research program, led by Aditya Ramesh (@model_mechanic), has evolved over the past year into OpenAI Robotics. Progress is rapid, and based on a foundation of co-design between robotics hardware and ML research.

If you love working hands-on across the robotics stack and want to build the future, please consider joining us. Send an email with your background and evidence of exceptional accomplishment to: robotics-recruiting@openai.com

High Conviction Portfolio

About a year ago, my nephew gathered all his savings, and I told him we were going to invest it in the best ideas we could find.

Over the past 12 months, stocks have come and gone from the portfolio, but I believe the current setup is the strongest it’s ever been. The portfolio is up roughly 143% over the last 12 months.

The biggest contributors so far have been $ZIM, $STAA, and $IMDX.

After adding a few recent positions, I’ve decided not to make many changes. I believe this portfolio has the potential to double again over the next 12 months.

The current top three holdings, where I see the most asymmetric opportunities, are $WGS, $ORCL, and $NOW.

There is no leverage and no options. The strategy is simple: make very concentrated bets and allocate at least 30% to my highest-conviction idea at the time.

Most of the current positions were established over the last two to four weeks. Barring a major change in fundamentals, I don’t expect to make many changes from here.

My nephew is young and doesn’t need the money anytime soon, so this is truly a long-term portfolio. I have no intention of trading these positions.

These are my highest-conviction ideas based on where I see the greatest risk/reward opportunities today. Because of concentration, this portfolio can experience significant drawdowns along the way.

It’s designed for someone young with a long time horizon, not for everyone.

thread_tweets.text:
The numbers are correct per Bloomberg Intelligence (cited in Cerebras’ April 2026 S-1). They cover Cerebras’ addressable slice of the AI inference market — not the full global total — projected to grow from ~$66B (2025) to $292B (2029) at 45% CAGR. This outpaces training infrastructure (~20% CAGR).

Thank you for your thoughts anyway.

Every investment strategy goes through drawdowns but if the process is sound, long-term compounding works. 

The worst thing one can do is to abandon a sound strategy during a drawdown and switch to what is currently hot and working (style drift). 

Patience + process > emotions.
